coredata.dc:title: Dual-channel probe of carbon dots cooperating with gold nanoclusters employed for assaying multiple targets
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Kuncheng
Wang, Shanshan
Wang, Yingyi
Miao, Hong
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (CD@Papain) from papain have been originally synthesized in aqueous solution along with a quantum yield of 6.2%, and showed cyan fluorescence at 435nm. Meanwhile, papain-templated Au nanoclusters (AuNCs@Papain) were prepared with the same precursor. On the basis of the two types of nanomaterials, CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain have been designed to assemble as one nanosensor (termed as CD-AuNCs@Papain) through a typical cross-linking reaction. Significantly, it not only emitted the dual-emission fluorescent signals with the same excitation, but also can assay multiple targets through exhibiting cyan, yellow, and red emission respectively. Thereby, H2O2, doxycycline and I- can be detected by CD-AuNCs@Papain. The mechanism of the fluorescence variations of CD-AuNCs@Papain: Au(0) of CD-AuNCs@Papain oxidized as Au(I) by H2O2, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CD-AuNCs@Papain and I-, the inner filter effect (IFE) occurring caused by tetracyclines. Meanwhile, the detection limits of H2O2, doxycycline and I- were obtained as 0.3nM, 0.2nM, and 0.6nM at a signal-to-ratio of 3, respectively. These results suggested that the nanoprobe here has provided the possibility for rapidly assaying multiple targets with the acceptable selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Au nanoclusters
Papain
Dual-channel
Multiple targets

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent images of assaying H2O2, doxycycline and I- by CD-AuNCs@Papain
Optimizing conditions for synthesizing CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain
Optimizing the assaying conditions for H2O2, doxycycline, I-
Principle of H2O2, doxycycline and I- quenching the fluorescence of CD-AuNCs@Papain
Detections of H2O2, doxycycline and I- by using CD-AuNCs@Papain

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of detecting H2O2, tetracyclines and I-.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CD@Papain, Inset: photographs of CD@Papain under daylight (a) and UV light (b); (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs@Papain, Inset: photographs of AuNCs@Papain under daylight (a) and UV light (b); (C) Rf values of CD@Papain(a), AuNCs@Papain(b), simple mixing of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain(c), CD-AuNCs@Papain(d) by TCL; (D) 5 portions of CD-AuNCs@Papain (1mg/mL AuNCs@Papain were linked with various concentrations of 0, 0.75, 1.5, 2.25 and 3.0mg/mL CD@Papain); (E) Fluorescence spectra of CD@Papain, AuNCs@Papain and CD-AuNCs@Papain.

(A)HR-TEM image of CD-AuNCs@Papain; (B) HR-TEM image of CD-AuNCs@Papain, Red circles and blue circles represent AuNCs@Papain and CD@Papain; (C) Crystalline lattices of CD-AuNCs@Papain; (D) XPS spectrum of CD-AuNCs@Papain; (E) High resolution C1s peaks of CD-AuNCs@Papain; (F) High resolution Au4f peaks of CD-AuNCs@Papain. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A)Fluorescence spectra of CD-AuNCs@Papain in the absence (pink) or in the presence (cyan) of 10μMH2O2, 1μM doxycycline (yellow) or 10mM I- (red); (B) Images of CD-AuNCs@Papain with different concentrations of H2O2, doxycycline and I- under UV lamp irradiation. Fluorescence responses of CD-AuNCs@Papain with (a) varying concentrations of H2O2 (2, 20, 200, 2000, 20,000nM), (b) varying concentrations of doxycycline (1, 5, 25, 125, 625μM) and (c) varying concentrations of I- (2, 20, 200, 2000, 20,000μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Au4f peaks of CD-AuNCs@Papain after the addition of H2O2; (B) FTIR spectra of CD-AuNCs@Papain (black line) and CD-AuNCs@Papain - I- system (red line); (C) Illustration of the PL emission from CD-AuNCs@Papain containing C-OH and C˭C groups; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of doxycycline (a) and fluorescence excitation (b) spectra of CD-@Papain.

head.ce:title: Dual-channel probe of carbon dots cooperating with gold nanoclusters employed for assaying multiple targets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Engineer Research Center of Chongqing Pharmaceutical Process and Quality Control, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Engineer Research Center of Chongqing Pharmaceutical Process and Quality Control, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (CD@Papain) from papain have been originally synthesized in aqueous solution along with a quantum yield of 6.2%, and showed cyan fluorescence at 435nm. Meanwhile, papain-templated Au nanoclusters (AuNCs@Papain) were prepared with the same precursor. On the basis of the two types of nanomaterials, CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain have been designed to assemble as one nanosensor (termed as CD-AuNCs@Papain) through a typical cross-linking reaction. Significantly, it not only emitted the dual-emission fluorescent signals with the same excitation, but also can assay multiple targets through exhibiting cyan, yellow, and red emission respectively. Thereby, H2O2, doxycycline and I- can be detected by CD-AuNCs@Papain. The mechanism of the fluorescence variations of CD-AuNCs@Papain: Au(0) of CD-AuNCs@Papain oxidized as Au(I) by H2O2, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CD-AuNCs@Papain and I-, the inner filter effect (IFE) occurring caused by tetracyclines. Meanwhile, the detection limits of H2O2, doxycycline and I- were obtained as 0.3nM, 0.2nM, and 0.6nM at a signal-to-ratio of 3, respectively. These results suggested that the nanoprobe here has provided the possibility for rapidly assaying multiple targets with the acceptable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), one of important reactive oxygen species and a medical disinfectant, usually stable at pH around 2–4 and widely plays critical roles in chemical, biological, clinical, environmental and food processes. Overload of H2O2 has resulted in serious health and environmental issues, and may cause various human central nervous system diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Luo et al., 2009; Mao et al., 2002). Accordingly, accurately detecting H2O2 at a low concentration shows great importance in environmental, clinical, and pharmaceutical fields. Likewise, tetracycline series are one of the most important antibiotic families. They are ranked as the second in production and worldwide usage as bacteriostatic antibiotics by inhibiting the formation of proteins in the bacterial cells. They show activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. To be specific, doxycycline, 4-(dimethylamino)−1,4,4a,5,5a,6,11,12a-octahy dro-3,5,10,12,12a-pentahydroxy-6-methyl-1,11-dioxo-2-naphthacene carboxamide monohydrate, is a member of tetracyclines containing β-diketonate configuration. It has a broad-spectrum antibacterial function and is preferred in the treatment of specific infections owing to its fairly reliable absorption and its long half-life, which permits less frequent dosage. Thus, it has been frequently introduced to treat sinusitis, syphilis and pelvic inflammatory disease (Hassani et al., 2008). Again, iodide is one of the essential micronutrient for human beings. It plays an important role for maintaining the normal function of thyroid gland, which can secrete thyroxine to promote the metabolism, growth and development for human (Zhong et al., 2010). Additionally, iodide as a kind of antithyroid medicines can both cure thyroid disorder and further reduce various diseases. Recommended daily allowance (RDA) of iodine for adults is 140–200μg/d (Gamallo-Lorenzo et al., 2005). Hence, monitoring the amount of I- in human bodies has been considered as one of key parts for human health.

Carbon dots (CD) are fascinating carbon materials due to their unique properties consisting of stable photoluminescence, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, lack of optical blinking, small size, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and satisfactory fluorescent performance (Baker and Baker, 2010; Wang et al., 2011b; Yang et al., 2009). Therefore, this kind of material has drawn considerable attentions in the fields of nanobiotechnology, biological labeling, photocatalysis (Li et al., 2010), sensing (Zhao et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2012), biomedicine (Wang et al., 2011a) and delivering drugs (Lin et al., 2005). In the past few years, two major approaches have been developed for synthesizing carbon dots. One way was known as the top down, consisting of electrochemical oxidation (Zhao et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2007), acidic oxidation (Dong et al., 2010), arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004) and laser ablation (Hu et al., 2009). For the other way, hydrothermal (Zhu et al., 2012), microwave (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013), and ultrasonic (Tan et al., 2012) defined as the bottom up, have been basically applied to synthesize carbon dots. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) that are seriously pernicious for human health and the environment, carbon dots exhibited fascinating properties and potentiality not only in theoretic but also in practical aspects. Especially, numerous carbon dots have been utilized to design fluorescent probes towards analysis purposes (Algarra et al., 2014; Da Silva and Goncalves, 2011). Noble metal nanoclusters (NCs) usually consist of several to dozens of atoms with properties regulated by their subnanometer dimensions (Wang et al., 2009; Jin, 2010). Emerging as one kind of interestingly fluorescent nanomaterials, fantastic characteristics of NCs, including satisfactory water solubility, high quantum-yield, outstanding catalytic properties, biocompatibility and excellent stability (Patel et al., 2008; Yeh et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2012a, b; Li et al., 2014), have attracted extensive attentions recently, potentiating them as novels candidate for biosensing (Guo and Wang, 2011; Shiang et al., 2009), biolabeling (Hui et al., 2009; Shang et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2012a,b), and catalysis (Vilar-Vidal et al., 2012). Consequently, numerous methods have been developed during the past decades for synthesizing NCs such as Au, Ag and Pt nanoclusters. Compared with quantum dots (QDs), AuNCs exist in ultra-small size with low toxicity. As a new type of fluorescent material, unique characteristics of AuNCs have recently attracted growing attentions, potentiating it as a satisfactory candidate for biosensing, catalysis, and imaging (Jin, 2010; Shang et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2007; Parker et al., 2010).

In this study, a simple and green method for preparing water-soluble CD@Papain has been successfully established. Importantly, this strategy for preparing CD@Papain by one-step treatment of papain was proposed for the first time. Besides, both the precursor of CD@Papain and the synthesis procedure were substantially environmental-friendly, facilitating their biocompatibility and more extensive applications. Subsequently, we synthesized the water-soluble AuNCs@Papain, and its quantum yield was determined to be 5.6%. Encouraged by the same excitation wavelength and different emissions of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain, we have pro-posed a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe by linking CD@Papain with AuNCs@Papain for visualizing and detecting H2O2, doxycycline and I- ( Fig. 1). Additionally, the mechanisms of CD-AuNCs@Papain assaying the multiple targets have been investigated in detail. To be specific, CD-AuNCs@Papain assays H2O2 due to the oxidation of Au(0), and the hydrogen bonds forming between CD-AuNCs@Papain and I- provided the possibility of sensing I-, and the occurrence of IFE effect by introducing tetracyclines lead to the fluorescent quenching (Zhai et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2016). Taken together, this designed sensor of CD-AuNCs@Papain has broadened ways for assaying multiple samples based on the dual-channel signals.

All the ions and papain were obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnonlog Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4), gentamicin, doxorubicin, cefalexin, ampicillin, penicillin, ciprofloxacin, lincomycin, tetracycline, doxycycline, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, 30% hydrogen peroxide (30% H2O2), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, USA). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), methylene chloride and methanol were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water, 18.25 MΩ. cm, produced with an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) was employed for the following experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with excitation slit set at 5nm band pass and emission at 10nm band pass in 1cm×1cm quartz cell. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were taken via a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200kV. Elemental and functional groups analysis were obtained by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). T6221-07 Silica gel plate type was purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). The quantum yields were recorded by using an Absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). Meanwhile, UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Functional groups analysis was achieved by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was used to measure various pH values of the aqueous solutions, and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) for blending kinds of solutions. The thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was purchased from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd (Gongyi, China).

Interestingly, CD@Papain was synthesized for the first time. In brief, 0.6g papain was added into 6mL ultrapure water. Then the solution was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After heating at 200℃ for 8h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. CD@Papain were centrifuged at 16 000rpm for 10min to remove large particles. Then, CD@Papain were ultrafiltrated by an Amicon Ultra-4 filter under centrifugation at 9000rpm for 15min and redispersed in 5mL of ultrapure water. The fluorescent carbon dots were collected by dialysis against deionized water though a dialysis membrane (300 MWCO) for 48h. Finally, the crude products were purified with a silica column chromatography using mixtures of methylene chloride and methanol as eluents. The powder of CD@Papain was obtained by lyophilisation, and furhter dissolved in ultrapure water with the final concentration of 10mg/mL. The CD@Papain prepared here was stored at 4℃ until use.

Differently, the AuNCs@Papain was prepared by a reported method (Chen et al., 2013). AuNCs@Papain were synthesized through the reduction of HAuCl4 by papain. Specifically, 0.15mL of HAuCl4 solution (100mg/mL) was mixed with 6mL of papain solution (17.5mg/mL) under vigorous stirring at 37℃. After 5min, 450μL NaOH (2M) solution was introduced, and the sample solution was further incubated at 37℃ for 6h. The AuNCs@Papain were centrifuged at 16 000rpm for 10min to remove large particles. Then, AuNCs@Papain were ultra-filtrated using an Amicon Ultra-4 filter under centrifugation at 9000rpm for 15min and redispersed in 5mL of 0.01M phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.4). AuNCs@Papain were collected by dialysis against deionized water though a dialysis membrane (300 MWCO) for 48h. Finally, the crude products were purified with a silica column chromatography using mixtures of methylene chloride and methanol as eluents. The powder of AuNCs@Papain was obtained by lyophilisation, and furhter dissolved in ultrapure water with the final concentration of 10mg/mL. AuNCs@Papain solution was stored at 4℃ until use.

CD-AuNCs@Papain were prepared as follows: 1.5mL of CD@Papain（10mg/mL) solution was mixed with 2.8mL of 0.01M phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.4), and a 152μL aqueous solution containing 10mg/mL Sulfo-NHS and a 46μL aqueous solution of 10mg/mL EDC were successively added, followed by incubation for 2h to allow the activation of the carboxyl group on the CD@Papain. Then, separation procedure was performed though a dialysis membrane (500 MWCO). And 0.5mL of AuNCs@Papain (10mg/mL) solution was introduced into the above solution and the mixture was incubated for 6h under constantly stirring at room temperature. CD-AuNCs@Papain were then ultra-filtrated using an Amicon Ultra-4 filter under centrifugation at 9000rpm for 15min and dispersed in 5mL of 0.01M phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.4). The powder of CD-AuNCs@Papain was obtained by lyophilisation, and furhter dissolved in ultrapure water with the final concentration of 10mg/mL. CD-AuNCs@Papain prepared here was stored at 4℃ until use.

Initially, CD@Papain, AuNCs@Papain, the mixture of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain, CD-AuNCs@Papain (5μL 10mg/mL each) were loaded on a suitable thin-layer chromatographic plate coated with a 0.25-mm layer of chromatographic silica gel mixture, which has been previously washed with methanol. Then, the spots were allowed to dry, and the chromatogram was performed in a freshly prepared solvent system consisting of methylene chloride, methanol and glacial acetic acid in a equilibrated tank. The separation process stopped until the solvent front moved about three-fourths of the length of the plate, and the plate was removed from the developing chamber. In the end, the solvent front was labelled followed by air-dry, and the images of the plates under 365nm UV light were recorded. Similarly, five portions of CD-AuNCs@Papain solutions with the same amount of 5μL (1mg/mL AuNCs@Papain were reacted with various concentrations of 0, 0.75, 1.5, 2.25 and 3.0mg/mL CD@Papain) were simultaneously subjected to the above chromatographic analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent images of assaying H2O2, doxycycline and I- by CD-AuNCs@Papain
ce:section.ce:para: The assay strategy based on CD-AuNCs@Papain for multiple targets including H2O2, doxycycline and I- have been proposed here. Firstly, 1mL of CD-AuNCs@Papain (10mg/mL) was separately introduced into 0.2mL varying concentrations of H2O2 (2, 20, 200, 2000, 20,000nM). The mixture was then incubated 1h and 30℃ at pH=7. Similarly, 1mL of CD-AuNCs@Papain (10mg/mL) was added into 0.2mL different concentrations of doxycycline (1, 5, 25, 125, 625μM) and 0.2mL varying concentrations of I- (2, 20, 200, 2000, 20,000μM) under the same condition. Simultaneously, all the vials of CD-AuNCs@Papain with three kinds of targets were further exposed in the cube UV-Lamp box, and their corresponding fluorescent images were recorded with UV lamp irradiation.

Various targets were introduced the solution of CD-AuNCs@Papain. In particular, 100μL of CD@-AuNCs@Papain (0.5mg/mL) was first pipetted a 1mL vial and 100μL of H2O2 (10−4 M) working solution was added. Subsequently, this mixture was diluted with 800μL of Milli-Q purified water and followed by vortexing thoroughly. Finally, the reaction was incubated at 37℃ for 1h and further subjected to the fluorescence measurements. Likewise, different metal ions including I-, Cl-, Br-, S2O3 2-, CO3 2-, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Co2+, Zn2+(10−4 M for each) were separately introduced into the CD@-AuNCs@Papain prepared here by the same way. Similarly, several antibiotics of gentamicin, doxorubicin, cefalexin, ampicillin, penicillin, ciprofloxacin, lincomycin, (10−4 M for each) and tetracycline, doxycycline, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, (10−6 M for each) were individually added into CD-AuNCs@Papain.

To get insight into the PL mechanism of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain, a series of characterizations have been successively performed. To be specific, a strong fluorescence emission peak was centered at 435nm as excited by wavelength of 370nm ( Fig. 2A). CD@Papain was slightly yellow under day light (Fig. 2A, photograph a) and emitted obviously cyan fluorescence (Fig. 2A, photograph b) under UV light (365nm). Meaningfully, the fluorescent solution has been preserved for more than 3 months without marked decay of fluorescence intensity, indicating their commercial potential (Fig. S1A, ESI†). The maximum excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized CD@Papain were recorded as 370nm and 450nm (Fig. S1B, ESI†). Next, the surface bonds of the synthesized CD@Papain were analyzed by FTIR. In brief, the peak at 3400cm−1 (Fig. S1C, ESI†) could be ascribed to the characteristic absorption of O-H stretching vibration while the C˭O stretching vibration peaks located at 1651cm−1, revealing the existence of carboxylic groups. Similarly, the peaks at 2889cm−1 were attributed to the N-H stretching vibration. Accordingly, the FTIR data demonstrated that CD@Papain were equipped with the characteristic functional groups including –OH, –COOH and–NH2. To directly view CD@Papain, HR-TEM analysis was further employed. CD@Papain possessed an average diameter in the range of 5.3±1.0nm (Fig. S2A, ESI†), describing their well-distributed size. Towards the surface composition and elemental analysis, CD@Papain was further characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. S2C and Fig. S3 (ESI†), the XPS survey spectrum indicated the presence of S, C, N and O in the product (four peaks at 167.2, 285.0, 399.3, and 532.4eV attributing to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s), indicating that CD@Papain prepared from papain are doped with N and S.

Analogously, AuNCs@Papain as described was investigated. In brief, a fluorescence emission peak was centered at 632nm as excited by the same wavelength of 370nm (Fig. 2B). To be specific, the quantum yields (5.6%) synthesized here is higher than that (4.3%) of AuNCs@Papain reported (Chen et al., 2013). Moreover, the quantum yield we got here is a little bit higher than that of other kinds of gold nanoclusters (Zhang et al., 2012a, b; Li et al., 2014). Thus, this quantum yield could be considered as satisfactory. AuNCs@Papain solution was obvious yellow under day light (Fig. 2B, photograph a), while it emitted a light-red fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 2B, photograph b). As depicted in Fig. S2B (ESI†), AuNCs@Papain existed as spherical dots with a diameter of 2.0±0.3nm. As revealed in Fig. S2D (ESI†), there were S, C, N, O, and Au of AuNCs@Papain. Four peaks at 167.6, 285.3, 400.3, and 532.2eV were attributed to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s (Fig. S4, ESI†). AuNCs@Papain solution also showed scarce change in emission spectrum upon storage for 3 months (Fig. S5A, ESI†). The FTIR data suggested that there existed the characteristic functional groups including –OH, –COOH and–NH2 on the surface of AuNCs@Papain (Fig. S5B, ESI†).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing conditions for synthesizing CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, a series of experiments were performed to explore the optimized conditions for synthesizing CD@Papain or AuNCs@Papain. As revealed in Fig. S6A, S6B, and S6C (ESI†), the fluorescence intensity of CD@Papain varied along with varying concentrations of papain, reaction time and temperature, describing that synthesis of CD@Papain depended on these selected conditions. Therefore, 100mg/mL of papain 6mL, 8h and 200℃ served as the optimal conditions for the synthesis of CD@Papain. Likewise, 17.5mg/mL of papain 6mL, 2M of NaOH 450μL, 8h and 37℃ served as the optimal conditions toward synthesizing AuNCs@Papain (Fig. S7A, S7B, S7C and S7D, ESI†).

To explore the property of CD-AuNCs@Papain, the thin-layer chromatography has been employed. Initially, CD@Papain(a), AuNCs@Papain(b), simple mixing of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain(c), CD-AuNCs@Papain(d) were loaded on the thin-layer chromatography (Fig. 2C). The four solutions showed different Retadation factor values (Rf values), and the Rf values of CD-AuNCs@Papain was between that of CD@Papain or AuNCs@Papain only, demonstrating that AuNCs@Papain can be linked with CD@Papain. Moreover, the linking density of CD@Papain and AuNCs@Papain was identified, and the fluorescence intensity of CD-AuNCs@Papain was gradually enhanced as the concentration of CD@Papain increased (Fig. 2D). Thus, the optimal linking ratio of synthesizing CD-AuNCs@Papain was selected, and the concentrations of CD@Papain versus AuNCs@Papain were 3mg/mL: 1mg/mL. As anticipated, CD-AuNCs@Papain displayed a dual-emission spectrum with two well fluorescence peaks, and one located at 435nm from CD@Papain as well as the other at 632nm originated from AuNCs@Papain (Fig. 2E). The two emission peaks were well separated by a wavelength distance of 197nm, potentiating a possible resolution for duel-channel detections. Also, the fluorescence intensity of CD-AuNCs@Papain solution showed little change upon storage for 3 months (Fig. S8A, ESI†).

In addition, the HR-TEM images revealed the sizes of particles become lager compared with that of CD@Papain or AuNCs@Papain only, indicating that the carbon dots and nanoclusters have been successfully linked together ( Fig. 3A and B). Again, the lattice spacing of 0.32nm agreed with that of the spacing between graphene layers (002 facet), and 0.24nm spacing was ascribed to the Au(111) (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, there exhibited the peak of Au along with the peaks of S, C, N and O in the XPS spectra (Fig. 3D). Meanwhile, the three major peaks referred to 284.5eV (sp2 graphitic C and sp3 graphitic C), 285.9eV (C-O, C-N, C-S), and 288.1eV (C˭N, C˭O) (Fig. 3E). Also, the two peaks of Au 4f7/2 corresponding to 83.5 and 84.2eV showed there existed Au(0) and Au(I) for CD-AuNCs@Papain (Fig. 3F). Additionally, the FTIR spectrum illustrated that CD-AuNCs@Papain were primarily equipped with the functional groups including –OH, –COOH and –NH2 (Fig. S8B, ESI†). Taken together, the above characterizations suggested that CD-AuNCs@Papain still preserved the similar composition and structure as individual CD@Papain or AuNCs@Papain.

Interestingly, dramatic decrease for the fluorescence of CD-AuNCs@Papain at 632nm was observed once H2O2 was introduced and there was scarce change of the fluorescence at 435nm ( Fig. 4A). In striking contrast, a fluorescent peak at 538nm evidently appeared accompanied with the disappearance of the fluorescent peaks at 435nm and 632nm when doxycycline was added ( Fig. 5A). Moreover, dramatic decrease for the fluorescence of CD-AuNCs@Papain at 435nm was observed by adding into I-, but the fluorescence at 632nm showed rare change (Fig. 5A). Taking the advantage of the multiple-responses for H2O2, doxycycline and I-, the CD-AuNCs@Papain designed here has provided the possibility for assaying the three targets. Thereby, CD-AuNCs@Papain was further employed for exploring the dual-channel assay. Firstly, five different concentrations of H2O2 were added into CD-AuNCs@Papain, gradual decrease of the red fluorescence and invariant cyan fluorescence were observed (Fig. 4B, photograph a). Likewise, the color of CD-AuNCs@Papain showed yellow from heavy to light under UV light as the concentration of doxycycline increased by degrees, however, no red and cyan fluorescence emerged during this process (Fig. 4B, photograph b). In like manner, dramatic decrease of the cyan fluorescence and unchanged red fluorescence for CD-AuNCs@Papain were observed upon introducing five varying concentrations of I- (Fig. 4B, photograph c). Taken together all the previous phenomenon, the current CD-AuNCs@Papain can be applied for detecting H2O2, doxycycline and I-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the assaying conditions for H2O2, doxycycline, I-
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the conditions of assaying H2O2, doxycycline and I- by CD-AuNCs@Papain, more experiments were performed. For detecting H2O2, the possible effect by incubation time, temperature and pH were investigated. As a result, 1h of incubation, 30℃ and pH=4 served as the final conditions for the following experiments as shown in Fig. S9A, S9B, and S9C (ESI†). In the same way, 1h of incubation, 30℃ and pH=7 were selected as the optimal conditions for detecting doxycycline (Fig. S10A, S10B, and S10C, ESI†). Again, 1h for incubation, 40℃ and pH=2 were considered as the optimal conditions for the following detections of I- (Fig. S11A, S11B, and S11C, ESI†). Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of CD-AuNCs@Papain scarcely exhibited variation along with different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S9D, S10D and S11D, ESI†), suggesting the satisfactory ability of this proposed method for bearing high ionic strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of H2O2, doxycycline and I- quenching the fluorescence of CD-AuNCs@Papain
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the mechanism for CD-AuNCs@Papain sensing H2O2, I-, and doxycycline, further explorations have been designed. For H2O2, the XPS spectra showed that the as-prepared CD-AuNCs@Papain contained only a small percentage of Au(I), which was involved in maintaining the stability of CD-AuNCs@Papain (Fig. 3F). In contrast, a larger percentage of Au(I) was observed as CD-AuNCs@Papain was subjected to the reaction with H2O2 (Fig. 5A). Thus, this fluorescence quenching was attributed to oxidation of the CD-AuNCs@Papain by H2O2 (Ju et al., 2014).

Next, the FTIR was employed for pursuing the mechanism of CD-AuNCs@Papain assaying I-. Particularly, the C–OH vibration of CD-AuNCs@Papain added with I- has shifted from 3400 to 3371cm−1 (29cm−1) compared with that of CD-AuNCs@Papain, suggesting the formation of hydrogen bonding, further leading to the fluorescence quenching at 435nm (Wang et al., 2013). This data indicated that the fluorescence quenching was attributed to molecular interactions (Fig. 5B).

When doxycycline was introduced, it would interact with the organic groups on the surface of the CD-AuNCs@Papain along with the appearance of absorption peak at 371nm (Fig. S12, ESI†), indicating that the structure of CD-AuNCs@Papain changed compared with that of CD-AuNCs@Papain or Doxycycline only (Wen et al., 2011). With the increase of C-OH and C˭C groups in Doxycycline-CD-AuNCs@Papain, the hydroxyl would reduce the energy level of the previous π* state (Hu et al., 2015) and the formation of conjugated system elevated the energy level of π state (Yan et al., 2009), thus facilitating to decrease the energy levels between π–π* gaps (Fig. 5C). Consequently, the decreased energy levels caused the emission wavelength to be stretched from 435nm to 538nm. Meanwhile, the disappearance of the fluorescence peaks at 632nm was owing to the decreased energy levels.

In addition, the inner filter effect will occur while the absorption bands of the quenchers possess an overlap region with the excitation or emission band of the fluorophores. Thereby, IFE will quench the fluorescence of the fluorophores, and the fluorescence quenching capacity depends on the concentration of the absorbent (Liu et al., 2013; Shao et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2014). Importantly, IFE occurring does not require any covalent interactions between the quencher and fluorophore. Herein, there is a coincident overlap at 370nm between the absorption band of doxycycline and the excitation band of CD-AuNCs@Papain (Fig. 5D). Simultaneously, doxycycline showed a higher molar extinction coefficient (ε=6534) than other antibiotics (Table S1, ESI†), resulting in a high IFE efficiency.

For identifying the selectivity of CD-AuNCs@Papain towards different targets, the related experiments were performed. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity at 632nm of CD-AuNCs@Papain with H2O2 was obviously quenched, and the fluorescent signal at 435nm of CD-AuNCs@Papain with I- evidently decreased. Howbeit, the fluorescence intensities by introducing other ions exhibited scarce change for both the red and cyan fluorescence, indicating that other ions did not interfere with the process of sensing both H2O2 and I- (Fig. S13A, ESI†). In striking contrast, a fluorescent peak at 538nm dramatically appeared for tetracyclines, and both the red and cyan fluorescence disappeared during this procedure (Fig. S13B, ESI†). Meanwhile, the fluorescence by adding into other antibiotics showed little change, suggesting that CD-AuNCs@Papain can selectively assay tetracyclines, and other antibiotics did not affect the analytical process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of H2O2, doxycycline and I- by using CD-AuNCs@Papain
ce:section.ce:para:
Furthermore, the corresponding calibration curves for H2O2, doxycycline and I- were built up with the optimum conditions. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity of CD-AuNCs@Papain (0.5mg/mL) F
0
-F decrease versus the logarithmic plots of H2O2 concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 1000nM) with its correlation coefficient of 0.980. Besides, the detection limit was 0.3nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, suggesting its sensitivity and promising (Fig. S14A and S14B, ESI†). Similarly, the fluorescence decrease of CD-AuNCs@Papain (0.5mg/mL) F
0
-F versus the logarithmic of doxycycline concentrations (0, 5, 20, 200, 500, 2000nM) along with a correlation coefficient of 0.987 and the detection limit was 0.2nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. S14C and S14D, ESI†). In addition, we have compared the detection limit of the current method with that of other nanomaterials-based assays by taking H2O2 as the example, and the corresponding results were listed (Table S2, ESI†). As been shown, it can be found that the H2O2 detection limit by the current CD-AuNCs@Papain is lower and satisfactory. Likewise, the fluorescence intensity of CD-AuNCs@Papain decreased along with increased amounts of I- added (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 100, 1000nM). In particular, the fluorescence intensity decrease of CD-AuNCs@Papain (0.5mg/mL) F
0
-F versus the logarithmic plots of I- concentrations along with a correlation coefficient of 0.996 (Fig. S14E and S14F, ESI†). Meanwhile, the detection limit of I- was obtained as 0.6nM at a signal-to-ratio of 3. Taken together, the data above demonstrated that the current strategy based on CD-AuNCs@Papain may broaden ways for multiple-assaying.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized the cyan fluorescent carbon dots originated from papain for the first time. The whole synthesis procedure was simple and environmental-friendly. Importantly, we have designed a dual-channel probe through CD@Papain cooperating with AuNCs@Papain, and further employed them for sensing multiple targets by exhibiting cyan, yellow, and red emissions. This strategy has showed an acceptable stability for detecting H2O2, doxycycline and I-, and covered the broad linear ranges with the satisfactory detection limits. Besides, the CD-AuNCs@Papain has also provided a high-performance visible platform for monitoring H2O2 doxycycline, I- under UV lamp. Significantly, the sensing mechanisms by CD-AuNCs@Papain for the three targets have been clarified in detail including Au (0) oxidized by H2O2, the formation of hydrogen bond between CD-AuNCs@Papain and I-, IFE occurring by tetracyclines. Overall, this assay has paved a new way for investigating multiple targets during single procedure and beyond.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2015A005), Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003), Innovative Research Project for Postgraduate Students of Chongqing (CYS16047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
